data.title: Inflation Still Over Target In August
data.description:
By William J. Luther

Inflation has exceeded the Fed’s two percent target for 54 consecutive months, with prices up 25 percent since March 2020.

Prices rose faster last month, according to new data from the Bureau of Economic Analysis. The Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index (PCEPI), which is the Federal Reserve’s preferred measure of inflation, grew at an annualized rate of 3.2 percent in August 2025, up from 2.0 percent in the prior month. It has averaged 2.2 percent over the last six months and 2.7 percent over the last year.

Core inflation, which excludes volatile food and energy prices but also places more weight on housing services prices, declined. According to the BEA, core PCEPI grew at an annualized rate of 2.8 percent in August 2025, down from 2.9 percent in July. It has averaged 2.5 percent over the last six months and 2.9 percent over the last year.

Inflation appears to be closer to the Fed’s two percent target when imputed - or, estimated - prices are excluded. Some prices, like those for owner-occupied housing and financial services furnished without payment (e.g., free checking accounts), are not directly observed and must be estimated for inclusion in the PCEPI.

But there is some debate about the quality of such estimates. Market-based PCE, which is a supplemental measure offered by the BEA based on household expenditures for which there are observable prices, grew at an annualized rate of 2.5 percent in August 2025. It has averaged 2.0 percent over the last six months and 2.4 percent over the last year.

Market-based core PCE, which removes food and energy prices in addition to most imputed prices, grew 1.9 percent in August 2025. It has averaged 2.3 percent over the last six months and 2.6 percent over the last year.

Fed officials seem inclined to attribute the recent uptick in inflation to higher tariffs imposed by the Trump administration, something monetary policymakers should ignore. Last week, the Federal Open Market Committee voted to lower the federal funds rate target range by 25 basis points, marking the first cut since December 2024.

FOMC members also signaled that additional rate cuts would soon follow. The median FOMC member projected the midpoint of the federal funds rate target range would decline another 50 basis points this year. That approach is sensible if one expects tariffs will have a temporary effect on inflation and little-to-no effect on inflation expectations.

“Goods prices, after falling last year, are driving the pickup in inflation,” Fed Chair Jerome Powell said earlier this week. “Incoming data and surveys suggest that those price increases largely reflect higher tariffs rather than broader price pressures.” He noted that prices for services continued to grow more slowly.

Powell also indicated that inflation expectations appear to be well anchored. Although inflation expectations for the next year “have moved up, on balance, over the course of this year on news about tariffs,” Powell said, “most measures of longer-term expectations remain consistent with our two-percent inflation goal.”

At the same time, Fed officials seem worried that tight monetary policy may be causing the economy to grow more slowly than it should. Powell said the “moderation in growth” observed in the first half of 2025 “largely reflects a slowdown in consumer spending.” He also said the labor market had become “less dynamic and somewhat softer,” with higher downside risks to employment.

All of this suggests the Fed will proceed with its plans to cut rates further this year, despite higher inflation. The CME Group reports the implied odds of an October rate cut at 89.8 percent. It reports a 97.2 percent chance that the federal funds rate will be at least 25 basis points lower following the December meeting, and a 65.0 percent chance the federal funds rate will be 50 basis points lower.

Original Post

data.title: Bloom Energy: The Not Too Overvalued Bet Fueling The Data Center CapEx Boom
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Sarfatti Investment Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

I am initiating coverage on Bloom Energy (NYSE:BE) with a Strong Buy rating. BE is very well positioned in today’s market to deploy its fuel cell solutions to support the rapid data center CapEx expansion fueled by AI. Further exacerbating BE’s growth prospects is the current natural gas-forward energy mix in the US, with little propensity to change in the medium term. My thesis will be focused largely on data centers, as this is where growth is concentrated, with a brief aside on maritime developments.

Background

Before diving into BE, it is necessary to gain some context on the underlying industry supporting BE’s rapid expansion in the next years. Nebius (NBIS) recently announced a $17.4 billion deal, with the option to reach $19.4 billion, with Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) to provide compute through its New Jersey data center over the next 5 years. Furthermore, CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) has over $30 billion in revenue backlog and contracts with big-ticket AI-centric customers like Microsoft, Mistral AI, OpenAI among others. In addition, Oracle (ORCL) has also signed a deal with OpenAI worth $300 billion to provide computing over 5 years starting from 2027.

The past few months have seen agreements, in addition to the recent ones mentioned above, worth hundreds of billions of dollars to gain access to compute power—with an innate property of being energy intensive. Additionally, many of these deals are resulting in greenfield data center development, with OpenAI's Stargate Project investing $500 billion into building new AI infrastructure in the US. Developing greenfield sites (fresh build-outs on undeveloped land) will allow developers to design data centers with the most energy-efficient and modern infrastructure.

Furthermore, McKinsey projects a +3.5x change in the total data center capacity demand for AI workloads alone. The expected capital expenditure to support this increase in demand is expected to be $5.2 trillion. McKinsey further segments this CapEx spend in another article with a $6.7 trillion scenario, implying a total spend on power generation worth $400 billion, or ~6% of overall CapEx spending.

Product Overview & Competitor Analysis

For this section, I am solely going to focus on BE’s solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC) offering and not touch their solid oxide electrolyzers (SOEC) because SOFCs are currently their flagship product, and they are going to be the key driver in continuing BE’s growth journey.

So, what are SOFCs? They are high-temperature ceramic fuel cells that electrochemically convert fuel to electricity, usually with high electrical efficiency (60%). These SOFCs reform hydrocarbons like natural gas, biogas, and syngas. The key value add with SOFCs is their high efficiency, compact form-factor, lower carbon footprint compared to incumbent solutions, and fuel flexibility. Within the fuel cell segment, SOFCs are most directly competing with proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cells. I compared them below. It is clear SOFCs are a moderately better choice for data centers given their generally higher efficiency and fuel flexibility but also easier availability of said fuels.

SMRs are being touted as the next big revolution for energy access in data centers. I don’t deny this, but SMRs are at a minimum 2+ years out from regulatory approval in an optimistic scenario, with some analyses suggesting mid to late 2030s for significant adoption. For a more near- to medium-term solution, SOFCs, in my opinion, are far more realistic.

It is also crucial to touch on the incumbent technology that these SOFCs are attempting to phase out—large sets of generators mostly made by companies like Caterpillar (CAT). CAT’s products are currently used within data centers for both primary and backup power. CAT’s overall energy and transportation business (which includes data centers but also other verticals) generated $28 billion in sales in 2024, accounting for about 44% of machinery-related sales.

So where does the value proposition of SOFCs over traditional generators come from? SOFCs cost more upfront, but result in a higher operational efficiency of about 30%, resulting in a net saving over usage lifecycles. SOFCs also have lower local emissions, a more compact form factor, faster time-to-market, and greater fuel flexibility, as previously mentioned. However, the key detracting factors include a less mature service footprint, a limited long-term track record, and a slower start in outages.

Thesis on US Energy Mix

Today, energy in the US is obtained largely through fossil fuels, with the two largest energy sources being petroleum and natural gas (used in the SOFCs).

Additionally, much of data center colocation growth, according to JLL, is concentrated towards low-cost electricity markets. In particular, states like Ohio, Texas, Virginia, Arizona, and Georgia are seeing data center development growth. This is largely driven by the fact that natural gas is abundant and cheap in the US. Most of the states where data center development is growing have a higher usage of natural gas than the national consumption rate of 36%, as shown below.

Data centers today are utilizing very high amounts of energy, consuming 4.4% of total electricity in the US in 2023, with estimates of up to 12% usage by 2028. According to the UCS, meeting this added demand through new, less fossil fuel-reliant sources requires more transmission lines, which can take 5-9 years to plan and build. This time horizon is too far out to meet current demands for compute. Data centers are left with no option but to utilize current energy infrastructure, currently heavily skewed towards natural gas. So, what does this mean for BE? SOFCs very efficiently utilize natural gas, and they propose a value proposition over traditional gensets, resulting in BE becoming very well positioned to realize this boom in data center CapEx.

Recent Developments

In July, BE announced a partnership with ORCL to power AI data centers within 90 days, piggybacking on ORCL’s $25 billion CapEx plan to expand AI infrastructure capabilities. BE also has aggressive plans to double manufacturing capacity to 2 GW by EoY 2025 by investing $100 million. American Electric Power Company, Inc. (AEP), one of BE’s current clients, is working on constructing a new natural gas pipeline to power a data center in Ohio, with an expansion order to BE expected soon. Additionally, on top of deals and announcements, BE is also able to execute 3 quarters in a row of record revenue and profits with $401 million in revenue in Q2 2025, a 19.5% increase quarter over quarter.

Additionally, BE will also have the capacity to diversify away from just data centers, with the American Bureau of Shipping approving Bloom Energy’s 65kW Marine Power Module on board ships. The International Maritime Organization, the UN agency responsible for developing and maintaining a comprehensive regulatory framework for shipping, will enforce sustainability measures for large ocean-going ships starting 2027 aiming for net-zero emissions by 2050. A.P. Møller - Mærsk A/S (OTCPK:AMKBY) also states that fuel cells could provide a reduction in certain emissions, further abetting a use-case of fuel cells to reduce emissions in maritime.

Risks

Although BE is seemingly well-positioned, there are multiple risks that would change my future outlook on the company. An execution risk exists, particularly with the ORCL deal, where bold claims of deployment within 90 days were made. Additionally, neither the size of the deal, nor a multi-year commitment was announced, signaling potential uncertainty on ORCL’s end and potentially unfavorable unit economics for BE. This risk is further intensified if BE is not able to materialize proposed cost reductions.

Another key risk dimension to consider is regulatory risk. Although BE’s SOFCs are more sustainable than incumbent solutions, they still face risk from evolving emissions regulations. This risk is amplified when considering BE operates internationally in geographies like the EU (notorious for strict sustainability regulations) and Japan.

Given BE operates in a very competitive market, new entrants & emerging technologies could pose cleaner & more energy-efficient solutions, which can also drive regulatory changes. Furthermore, industrial customers like factories & data centers could have charges imposed on them by regulators for grid defection by utilizing distributed generation—the practice of producing electricity at localized power sources as opposed to being connected to the centralized power grid. BE’s business model relies heavily on industrial customers choosing BE to decrease grid dependence.

Valuation

There has been lots of discussion on BE being overvalued. I present a contrarian view where I believe there is lots of room for growth for BE. In the above table is a list of companies comparable to BE and their corresponding multiples. I think that in today’s markets it is not sound to use LTM multiples for valuation, especially for growth companies like BE, where much of the revenue & EBITDA growth is concentrated towards the future. Many deals, such as the recent one with ORCL, are not factored into revenue yet, but are accounted for in share price.

Looking at NTM multiples, BE actually trades at a discount to similar high-growth companies with lots of room for future growth and deals, given the rapid expansion in data center CapEx. Yes, it can be argued that the companies I have selected are overvalued themselves, but many present a similar profile to BE, with NBIS having strong forward-looking momentum with their Microsoft deal and CRWV presenting a similar case with their backlog.

Above is a sensitivity table showing how implied share price changes under different assumptions for revenue multiples. The price target is derived by multiplying revenue by the target multiple to obtain enterprise value, which is then adjusted by net debt to arrive at equity value, which is then divided by total fully diluted shares outstanding to reach the share price. For my PT, I am using the median of TEV/Revenue NTM multiples from the previous table of 9.0x, resulting in a 12-month price target of $166. I am expecting margin expansion over the next quarters driven by investments in increasing manufacturing capacity to increase margins. I also expect further revenue growth driven by higher adoption of SOFCs in data centers, exacerbated by the current energy infrastructure situation in the US, which is not due to revolutionize in the medium-term.

Conclusion

To conclude, BE is well positioned to take advantage of the opportunity created by a strong increase in data center CapEx driven by the high compute demands of developing AI solutions. BE's SOFCs propose a strong value proposition over incumbent solutions, and any real threats like SMRs are multiple years out. Further strengthening BE's positioning is the natural gas forward energy infrastructure in the US. Although there are real risks like regulatory changes and failure to execute, I believe that BE has a strong growth journey ahead and offers investors an attractive entry point to one of the key enablers in fueling AI.

data.title: Apple: I Was Wrong In July; That's Why I'm Switching To Buy Today
data.description:
Mea culpa

During the journey of my career, I have gradually understood to be humble and to be aware of my mistakes and consequently admit them. Where I come from, they say, “If you don't work, you don't make mistakes,” but I don't mean to justify myself with that. As I mentioned, I completely miscalculated the situation with Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) in July (Apple Q3: $100 Billion In Buybacks, $9 Billion In CapEx - Why I'm Not Buying). I have always been a fan of the brand, both as a consumer of the products and services that it offers and as a long-term investor, but in July I was convinced that the stock would have to stay at a plateau, at least for a period.

I had worries regarding the overpriced assessment of the stock and the hints of deceleration and lack of visibility on the AI issue it was suffering from, which made me feel upset. Apple has been a lot more durable than I expected for the last two months, so at this point, I am changing my decision. I am now promoting Apple to a "buy" (in the long term). I will lay out a number of factors for this alteration of view, which I will expound on in this analysis.

Overview

The company certainly needs no introduction. It is one of the largest tech companies in the world, with a business dominated by a product that no one can do without anymore, namely the iPhone (about half of its revenue comes from this), even though over the years it has been able to diversify with great success. The services division has a gross margin of around 74%, almost double that of the hardware division (around 37%).

Wearables, Home and Access 37 9%

iPad 27 7%
Total Revenue 391 100%

From a financial point of view, it generates impressive cash flow. Every year, it generates tens of billions in free cash flow, most of which is returned to shareholders through a regular and aggressive buyback policy. Just to give you an idea, in the last decade alone, the company has repurchased over $900 billion worth of its own shares (see table below, source: Bloomberg Terminal) and, in the meantime, pays quarterly dividends (albeit modest ones of around 0.5%).

Why I was pessimistic in July

Stretched valuation and slowing growth

Usually, the first thing I look at is multiples, and for my taste, the stock's valuation was (and still is today) a bit stretched. On the other hand, it is also true that we are getting used to this new era in which high valuations seem almost the new normal for the markets. Be that as it may, the P/E ratio is currently 35x earnings, and EV/EBITDA is 25x, which is very high compared to the historical average. Normally, these values are fine for a fast-growing tech company, but less so for a company like Apple, which was beginning to show signs of slowing down.

Macro and geopolitical risks

The current macroeconomic context is not the best. Apple in particular is in a complicated situation with China because it is both a market where it sells (17% of its revenue comes from there) and an extremely important production base. My fear was that at a delicate time like this, with the two countries engaged in a trade war, Apple could be one of the targets. We have already seen something like this in the past. Remember 2023? The Communist Party banned the use of iPhones by all government employees and officials. Apple also has some problems in the West, particularly with the European Community, whose strict regulations seem to represent a difficult obstacle to overcome.

CapEx at a minimum, buybacks at a maximum

The heart of the matter? The main reason I recommended “sell” in my previous analysis is the capital management; in fact, in the last quarter, Apple spent $9.4 billion on capex and $22.9 billion on buybacks, which is almost triple its productive investments. For me, this aspect is really very important. We are in a market phase where the first to arrive wins (I'm talking about the AI race). I am very interested in this aspect and have already discussed it when analyzing Meta (Meta: $70 Billion Later, The Real AI Winner Is Here), Microsoft (Microsoft Q4 Preview: AI Is Not A Gamble, It's Already Helping Margins), and Alphabet (Alphabet Spends $85 Billion To Build The Future, Wall Street Panics - I Buy). Simply put, Meta spends over $40 billion on AI and data centers, Alphabet talks about a super spending cycle, and Microsoft is the main financier of OpenAI, but in reality, it is at the center of the whole mechanism.

So my question is: where is Apple? The world's most innovative company seems to me to be cornered, waiting to make its move. It seemed distracted by inflating EPS with buybacks but not securing its future by investing in AI.

What has changed since July?

Among the new positive catalysts, I would put the release of the new iPhone at the top of the list, which, compared to expectations, seems to be in high demand. According to Ming-Chi Kuo's analysis (for those who don't know him, he is a long-standing Apple expert), demand for the iPhone 17 seems to be robust, and I quote:

“Production of the iPhone 17, 17 Pro, and 17 Pro Max for the third quarter of this year is about 25% higher than their predecessors last year, along with increased pre-order demand for all three models.”

The absolute novelty of this generation is the iPhone Air, which, regardless of whether you like the design or not, is designed to offer a “full screen” experience at lower prices than the Pro. Apple has essentially added a fourth model to the lineup (17, 17 Pro, 17 Pro Max, and Air) to better cover various price ranges and embrace a wider range of consumers

  • The base model iPhone 17 now has features previously reserved for the Pro (such as 120Hz ProMotion and Always-On display, according to MacRumors).
  • The Pro models retain differences (materials, better cameras) that make them preferable to advanced users.
  • The new Air could win over those who want a large screen but don't need multiple cameras.

I like this strategy because it shows me how Apple is starting to pay more attention to maximizing ARPU (average selling price) by pushing the Pro models, but at the same time trying not to lose price-sensitive customers by offering the Air.

On the AI issue, however, I remain somewhat perplexed because, while it is true that something is happening, I still find it difficult to see it as a concrete lever in the short term. I consider it more as a sort of “call option,” so if it arrives, it will improve the current situation. I expect and hope that Apple will keep pace with the other big players, but I am certain that real monetization will come later: for now, the bulk of the story remains the iPhone and services, certainly not AI.

Warning: high multiples

Let's move on to the sore point: the numbers. I'll say it right away, Apple is not cheap, and there's no doubt about that. As I said, the P/E ratio is around 35 times earnings, and the EV/EBITDA is around 25x. We are at or near historic highs, so it is reasonable to ask, “If I buy today, am I buying at a high price?” The answer I can give you is that Apple deserves at least part of this premium valuation, and the improved outlook reduces the risk of a drastic compression of multiples.

At one time (pre-2016), it was priced as a cyclical hardware company, with a P/E ratio of around 12-15x.

Today, we cannot consider Apple solely as a hardware company, as we did in the past, but rather as a consumer company that you cannot do without. Its user base is huge and loyal, guaranteeing recurring cash flows. This is probably why the market values it at higher multiples than in the past. The low interest rates we have experienced in recent years of monetary easing have also contributed to this. In fact, if we look at the period between 2019 and 2021, the average P/E ratio first rose above 20x during this period, reaching and almost exceeding 45x. Today, we are far from those levels, but we remain at the high end of the historical range, just below the all-time highs.

However, the quality is there, and as I said, it should be rewarded. It is one of the top companies in the S&P 500 index with the highest ROIC, at around 50%, with a competitive advantage that is difficult to undermine (integrated hardware-software-services ecosystem, enormous brand strength, and consumer loyalty at the highest levels), and above all, stable earnings and cash flow, which is not always easy to find in tech stocks.

This justifies a premium.

The company can therefore afford to return tens of billions a year to shareholders and invest as much as it needs in R&D (and let's hope it's enough to stay in the race!). The buybacks (which I viewed negatively in my July analysis because they seemed disproportionate to AI investments) are the most massive around. The float is reduced by an additional 2-3% each year, which alone adds a couple of percentage points of EPS growth without the need to increase total earnings. I'm not saying that Apple is undervalued, because it clearly isn't. I'm just saying that in a well-diversified, long-term portfolio, it makes sense to pay a premium for this type of quality. Apple's valuation remains a risk, but in the risk/return balance, I am now more inclined to accept it given the renewed momentum of the business.

Services and AI

Apple has a strength that not everyone has fully understood, namely a huge customer base—we're talking about over a billion people. It already exploits this “audience” in part through payment services (Apple Pay) and content (Apple TV+), etc. It has also recently entered the advertising sector, where Apple is quietly building an advertising business (ads on the App Store, Apple News, and potentially Apple Maps). If it decides to push into this field—always with caution so as not to damage its privacy reputation—it could tap into a portion of the huge digital advertising budgets. The company has an audience of over a billion premium users; there are many ways to better monetize this audience, and Apple has only explored a few of them so far. Each new successful service adds subscribers and ARR (annual recurring revenue). I believe it is possible that within 2-3 years, Services will exceed $120-$130 billion per year, approaching 30% of revenues. This would also bring Apple's operating margin to new highs (today it is around 30%, it could rise to 33-35% thanks to the richer mix). An interesting, hidden advantage.

The sore point, which, however, becomes a sort of call option on the stock, is Artificial Intelligence. Please don't hold it against me, but I still have some doubts, and I'm not convinced that AI will deliver results in the short term. I'm skeptical. Nevertheless, I see untapped potential. As a consumer and Apple user, I would be willing to pay for an additional premium service if the company offered AI services. We know that Apple customers are willing to pay for quality and privacy, and offering a reliable and private AI assistant could create a new revenue stream. I must say that I am eagerly awaiting this moment, as it is one of the areas where AI would make the most sense. For now, Siri is not up to speed, and it is time for them to wake up to this fact.

I'm back

From a technical analysis perspective, I am therefore starting to accumulate at these prices, aware that I am not getting the deal of the century given the levels. But I will start with small amounts. I would be more confident about entering on a slight downturn, in the $243 area (-5% from current levels). In that area, I would step on the accelerator with more confidence.

data.title: Core PCE Inflation Holds Steady At 2.9% In August, As Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the core PCE price index, rose 2.9% year-over-year in August, the highest level since February. This was consistent with the forecast and a slight pickup from July. On a monthly basis, the core index was up 0.2%, as expected.

The headline index was up 2.7% year-over-year, the highest level since April 2024. This was consistent with the forecast and marked an acceleration from June's 2.6% reading. On a monthly basis, the index was up 0.3%, as expected.

PCE Price Index

Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) measures and tracks changes for all domestic personal consumption. Core PCE measures the changes in personal consumption less food and energy, making it less volatile than the headline PCE. The PCE Price Index is calculated using PCE data and is a key way to measure changes in purchasing trends and inflation.

The adjacent thumbnail gives us a close-up of the trend in YoY core PCE since January 2012. The first string of red data points highlights the 12 consecutive months when core PCE hovered in a narrow range around its interim low. The second string highlights the lower range from late 2014 through 2015. Core PCE shifted higher in 2016 with a decline in 2017, 2019, and 2020, with a major jump in 2022.

The following chart shows the monthly year-over-year change in the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) price index since 2000. An overlay of the core PCE price index is also included, with the highlighted line on the chart representing the 2% benchmark for core inflation.

For a long-term perspective, here are the same two metrics spanning five decades.

Inflation: Fed's 2% Target

The Fed traditionally uses the PCE Price Index as their preferred inflation gauge; however, another common measure of inflation is the Consumer Price Index.

In their latest meeting, the Fed implemented a widely anticipated 25 basis point cut to the federal funds rate (FFR), bringing it to a range of 4.00%-4.25%. This marked the first rate cut of the year, with two more quarter-point cuts anticipated before year-end.

The statement from the meeting revealed the committee believes "inflation has moved up and remains somewhat elevated," but that they are strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective.

data.title: Pop Mart: Opportunity Or Passing Trend?
data.description:
Introduction

Unless you've been living under a rock for the past two years, Pop Mart (OTCPK:PMRTY) needs no introduction. Its financial results and stock performance have been staggering, mainly due to the tremendous success of Labubu, one of the company's IPs, which went viral internationally.

In this article, I'd like to delve deeper into Pop Mart's business model, analyze its financial performance, and try to understand whether it still represents a good investment opportunity, or whether it's too late to jump on the bandwagon.

Business Model

Pop Mart is a Chinese company founded in 2010, focused on creating proprietary IP and exploiting it through the production of collectible gadgets and toys and marketing them through an omnichannel strategy, which includes physical stores, vending machines, online channels and pop-up events.

The creation and management of new IPs are at the core of Pop Mart's business. In 2024, the group's performance was exceptional, with four of its IP portfolio exceeding RMB 1 billion in revenue for the first time, including: THE MONSTERS, MOLLY, SKULLPANDA, and CRYBABY. Another 13 IPs exceeded RMB 100 million in revenue. Pop Mart also creates products that leverage third-party IPs through collaborations aimed at attracting new customers interested in that specific IP and maintaining brand appeal.

The flagship of the portfolio is THE MONSTERS, which includes the aforementioned Labubu, with revenues exceeding RMB 3 billion in 2024. Labubu have become an international viral phenomenon, contributing to a huge volume of plush toy/accessory sales, and even becoming a fashion item. The blind box marketing model for these products, and the difficulty in some cases of purchasing the products directly from Pop Mart, has led to the creation of a secondary market, where these toys are sold at prices far above their original retail price. Through the blind box model, the buyer purchases a mystery box containing one of the various versions of the series, without knowing which one. This encourages repeat purchases by collectors who want to complete the series, and also creates a gamification effect, fueled by the hope of finding the rarest Labubu, which command a higher secondary market value. It's no secret that sales of these products are largely driven by the hope of finding the rarest versions and then reselling them on the secondary market.

Pop Mart relies on an omnichannel distribution network, divided into: offline, online, wholesale, and experiential channels. In mainland China, physical channels (single-brand stores and roboshops) remain key: in 2024, offline sales in the Mainland amounted to RMB4.5 billion, with 401 retail stores and 2,300 roboshops in operation. In international markets, the leap was much stronger, with offline sales of RMB3.1 billion in 2024 and 130 stores (including joint ventures/partner stores).

Regarding the online channel, Pop Draw generated approximately RMB1.1 billion in China and represents the largest share of the domestic online channel; Tmall and DouYin were significant drivers with RMB627.8 million and RMB601.2 million, respectively. Abroad, Pop Mart relies on its official website and external partners such as Amazon to achieve maximum geographic coverage. In 2024, sales from the official foreign website grew by 1,246% compared to 2023. Finally, we have the "wholesale & others" segment, which includes the POP LAND theme park. Overall, this segment has less weight than the other channels but is a good lever for the exploitation and dissemination of the IP.

The 2024 annual report also provides details on gross margins in the two geographical segments considered by the company. While in Mainland China the gross margin is 63.9% (66.4% offline; 64.1% online), in the rest of the world the gross margin reaches 71.3% (72.3% offline; 73.6% online). Consequently, international growth favors margin expansion.

An initial analysis clearly shows that the main risk lies in the concentration of the portfolio and dependence on a few IPs, which are strongly influenced by trends that can easily change. Funko could be the perfect example of a company that a few years ago produced products that everyone wanted and is now experiencing difficulties due to a change in consumer preferences.

Financial Performance

The group's results in the first half of 2025 were excellent. Revenue reached RMB13.9 billion, up 204.4% compared to the same period in 2024, while net profit jumped to RMB4.7 billion (+385.6%). Mainland China continues to be Pop Mart's largest market, with RMB8.28 billion in revenue, up +135% yoy, accounting for 59.7% of the group's total revenue. However, the most disruptive growth was seen in international markets. Asia Pacific (excluding China) generated RMB2.85 billion, up +258%, followed in terms of turnover by the US and the Americas, which generated RMB2.26 billion, more than ten times the turnover of the previous year. Performance in Europe was also extraordinary, with growth of +729% and revenues reaching RMB478 million.

In the United States, expansion has been rapid, with the number of stores increasing from 10 to 41 in twelve months; in Europe, Pop Mart has focused on iconic locations and cultural capitals to build awareness and has strengthened its online presence. In China, offline sales reached RMB5.08 billion, thanks to 443 physical stores and 2,437 roboshops, which alone generated over RMB678 million. The excellent performance of online channels in China increased the share of revenue generated by this channel, which accounted for 35.5% of Chinese sales (26.7% in the previous year). The share of foreign revenues rose to over 40%, with particular emphasis on the American market, which in just a few quarters has become a mainstay. To conclude, in the first half of 2025, the operating margin was over 43%, with operating income of RMB6 billion, up 436% compared to 2024.

Valuation

When it comes to evaluating Pop Mart, the question everyone should be asking is whether this extraordinary growth will continue. Clearly, sustaining triple-digit growth year after year is unsustainable, so it is reasonable to expect a significant slowdown.

Analysts expect growth to continue in 2026 and 2027, with revenues increasing by 28.8% and 24.6% respectively. They therefore do not seem to think that this is a passing fad, destined to fade away in the coming years. Personally, I find it difficult to imagine that Pop Mart will be able to maintain these growth rates in the future. As mentioned above, Funko (FNKO) can serve as a clear example of a “toy” or “collectible” that has moments of hype with rapidly increasing sales and moments when people are no longer interested in the brand, perhaps because there is a more popular alternative on the market. Given Pop Mart's heavy dependence on trends, I find it impossible to make projections beyond five years, and honestly, I can't say whether everyone will still be crazy about Labubu or Pop Mart's other IPs in 2030.

I therefore decided to carry out an assessment based on three scenarios. In all three scenarios, I retained the analysts' estimates for turnover and net profit up to 2027; I then made my own estimates, which vary according to the scenarios. In the base case scenario, I assumed continued growth, albeit at a more moderate pace, while in the worst case scenario, I considered the possibility of a “passing fad,” assuming a decline in revenues due to a change in consumer preferences. In the best-case scenario, I manipulated growth and margin estimates to understand what expectations would justify the current market valuation, a sort of reverse DCF. In all three scenarios, I considered a payout ratio of 15% and a discount rate of 10%.

Starting with the best-case scenario, Pop Mart's current market capitalization of approximately RMB344 billion would be justified if it managed to grow after 2027 by 15% in 2028 and 2029 and by 10% in 2030, with a net margin of 35% and a P/E ratio of 26.

Personally, I believe these expectations are very optimistic. In fact, I don't think we currently have enough data to determine whether Pop Mart IPs are a passing fad or something that is here to stay, like Pokémon or other pop phenomena. Furthermore, even if Pop Mart were to achieve these results, based on current prices, we can only expect a “normal” return of 10%, without any particular margin of safety.

In the other two cases, I use lower estimates, and as a result, Pop Mart would currently appear to be well above its intrinsic value.

In the base case, I assumed steady growth of 8% after 2027 and a P/E ratio of 20 in 2030. In the worst case, however, I assumed a 5% decline in revenue after 2027 until 2029, followed by zero growth in 2030. In the base case, the intrinsic value of the company would be approximately RMB212 billion, while in the worst case it would be RMB117 billion.

Conclusion

In conclusion, what Pop Mart has achieved with Labubu is a very interesting phenomenon. Recent financial results have been exceptional in terms of both revenue and margins, largely thanks to expansion into international markets.

The current price reflects very high growth expectations, and the continued success and popularity of Pop Mart products seems to be taken for granted. However, the main risk remains its dependence on a limited number of IPs and the volatility of consumer tastes.

History is full of examples of products that were successful for a few years and then forgotten, especially those that also serve as fashion gadgets. Funko's recent history can serve as a reminder that the line between a brand destined to stand the test of time and a passing fad can be very thin. The difficulty in estimating Pop Mart's future results therefore leads me to believe that this is not a suitable company for long-term investors with a moderate risk profile.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ARKW: Curb Your Enthusiasm, This ETF Is Up 100% But Not That Unique
data.description:
Before I explain my bottom-line thesis on the ARK Next Generation Internet ETF (BATS:ARKW), I encourage you to review this article part of Seeking Alpha's ETF education series. It contains a lot of the basics of the ETF that is the subject of this article.

But my thesis, while directly applicable to ARKW, applies to many ETFs in this manic market, where what a stock does or might do or hints at doing is enough to add billions in market cap. And by association, millions in assets under management. I'm happily profiting on the mayhem, as many are in 2025. But that doesn't mean I'm not still prioritizing risk management at all times.

And so it is with an ETF like this one, that I can both acknowledge its near-double in value during the first 3 quarters of 2025, and also point out some features of the fund that to me, make it less than meets the eye.

I know that the inclination from many self-directed investors in a go-go market like this is to see an ETF double, and instantly be more attracted to it. My input on that: anything can go up, or keep going up. ARKW might have just made its first 100% gain since the end of 2024, with more to go. But as a risk manager, I automatically reduce my enthusiasm when a security does that.

However, what is more important to me, in all cases but this one especially, is to understand WHY it is doing so well. Bull runs like this are part fundamental, part technical and often very emotion/sentiment-driven. I can't look at what's happened with ARKW this year and conclude "good for those who have enjoyed it, but don't use the past as prologue."

So, what's the answer to the pop quiz? These ETFs are all different tilts on the same tech/crypto theme that has been THE area to generate high returns through the first 9 months of this year. But when we look deeper into ARKW, we can see that it is more a beta play on the "tech good, others bad" mantra.

These lines all move in sync. Because they are all driven by the same lead factors. That ARK has been able to sell at least 4 ETFs that overlap notably in holdings, but repackage them as if they were different forms of "innovation" is frankly rich. But it is exactly what has made a lot of folks in the product sales business rich for decades. First in mutual funds, and now in ETFs.

Along the way, investors have done well too. But not consistently. And based on what I've seen in past cycles, this has the air of a "chase" higher. How long will it go on? Heck if I know. But I know to be skeptical when a fund gets hot like this. Particularly when it is so highly correlated to many others in the same "crowded trade."

Here's ARKW in isolation and with the 1-year rolling returns from above shown as a daily rolling figure. Post pandemic, this ETF roared higher by well over 200% over 12 months. So this is not even a record. In fact it accomplished a double during 2023. Something about odd-numbered years, I guess.

There's also the flip side, which we will see again to some degree. That's where ARKW loses 80% of its value. When an ETF shows me it is capable of that, I take notice. Because it all relies on the continued enthusiasm for what's been working. That is as much a factor of liquidity flows and broader market sentiment than anything else. I'm looking forward to these innovations changing our lives for the better, too. I'm just not counting on the wild projections and intense capital demands to deliver that long-term sustained growth. This is very dot-com-bubble like.

As is this table below. That's the near-full correlation (close to 1.00) between those ARK ETFs and the Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ). There's beta in ARKW, but the alpha is very hit and miss over time.

ARKW holdings analysis

The holdings here are a who's who of "innovation" stocks, but they are also held in other ARK ETFs to varying degrees. The issue I have is not the companies themselves, but the market's insistence on trading them like one big block, at least when the big moves occur. This is why ARKW, while the most successful of the bunch at times, is not doing something revolutionary. The differentiation is in the volatility, not the stock selection over time. Because these stocks have little chance of saving the portfolio when the liquidity cycle and market sentiment turns. In the meantime, party on!

With a portfolio selling at nearly 50x trailing earnings, what could possibly go wrong? A lot. That return on equity stands out as well. Negative. That translates to "priced for perfection." I don't invest based on such lofty odds. That's also why I don't get many 100% winners, outside of my options trading (where it the objective in many cases, albeit with very different risk profile involved).

Earnings and sales growth have been superb for this set of stocks. That's really what ARK is striving for. My concern is that the market has an on/off relationship with that, and so while ARKW might look like a portfolio, it is really more like the QQQ on steroids. If an investor is OK with that, then they can consider the extreme risk here.

This is what drove the near-double in ARKW so far this year. Perhaps the microcosm is ORCL, which just rallied in a way that is likely to reverse itself quickly, even more than it already has. Overshoots are common here, and drive returns for a while. But that excitement doesn't often translate to sustainable alpha.

There's plenty of overlap in the holdings to go around here, as shown below.

And when it comes down to it, if QQQ tanks, ARKW will tank too. And more recently, by a factor of "many," perhaps due to the increased crypto position (via ARK's own ETF in that space) and because it is exploring deeper into the innovation genre, where it will undoubtedly find high-flyers that are all systems go...until they crash.

Concluding thoughts on ARKW and risk management in 2025

As usual, it is easy for me to write and analyze a high flyer like ARKW. It is much tougher to "rate" it. I would not touch it, but I am also extremely comfortable with seeing it double in price again, and not get any FOMO. I prefer using options in small size to take my "big shots" with small amounts of money. So ARKW is a sell for me, simply because if I owned it (I don't and have not), it would be in the process of being reduced significantly in position Size.

Enjoy the ride if you like, but be careful out there.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: Enjoy A 4.2% Dividend Yield And Predictable Cash Flows
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) is the largest natural gas midstream company in North America, effectively making it the largest gas streaming company in the world. As part of my expanding coverage of oil and gas infrastructure companies, I am adding Kinder Morgan to my coverage. The company’s pipeline and storage network is a critical backbone of North America’s energy system, transporting roughly 40% of the U.S. natural gas supply to power plants, industrial users, and LNG export terminals. I believe the stable fee-based cash flows and an attractive yield of 4.2% make an attractive case for a company exposed to surging demand for natural gas. In this report, I analyze the business model, strategic positioning, and valuation to assess whether the stock really presents an attractive "Buy" opportunity from a fundamental perspective.

Kinder Morgan: Big In Natural Gas

What I like particularly about Kinder Morgan is their significant exposure to natural gas. The company has 55,000 miles of natural gas pipelines used to move 40% of the U.S.'s natural gas production. Natural gas prices are currently quite high, which I believe is a positive for Kinder Morgan. As I discuss in this report, the commodity price is not a major driver of the results, but a high natural gas price does signal strong demand, which also means strong demand for streaming the commodity. By 2030, Kinder Morgan expects demand for natural gas to increase 28 billion cubic feet per day, or BCFD. Wood Mackenzie is more conservative, expecting 22 BCFD, but that still points at 20% growth, while Kinder Morgan forecasts 25% growth. Both forecasts are strong.

For Refined Products, Kinder Morgan is the largest independent refined products transporter and terminal operator with 9,500 miles of refined products and crude oil pipelines, terminals, and a fleet of 16 Jones Act Tankers.

Nine percent of Kinder Morgan’s business consists of the transport of carbon dioxide. That also provides a future growth opportunity beyond growing demand for natural gas. Part of the energy transition story includes carbon capture and storage. Kinder Morgan can already transport carbon dioxide. Some investing in carbon capture link that to the transportation layer for storage as a growth opportunity, and through a joint venture with the Southern Ute Indian Tribe Growth Fund, the company is also working on providing transportation of carbon dioxide for storage in a well in the Permian Basin.

By extension, Kinder Morgan also sees opportunities for renewable natural gas, which also runs separately from fossil fuel cycles as it uses methane that would otherwise not be used. It would simply be emitted. The challenge for Kinder Morgan and any company that tries to capitalize on renewable natural gas through methane capture is that not all spots are economically viable. So, the company is mostly about natural gas transportation, refined oil products to a lesser extent, and an even smaller portion is focused on carbon dioxide and renewables. However, I believe that this is also where the company has opportunities through carbon capture, carbon storage, methane capture, and enhanced oil recovery, like they are already doing today.

Kinder Morgan’s revenue mix has transformed substantially over the past 10 years. The company's carbon dioxide business has reduced by 10 points, while the refined products business remained steady, accounting for 26% of the revenues. The total natural gas business, however, has grown from 54% to 64%, driven by natural gas transportation and storage. I believe this is a very good development given the increasing demand for natural gas.

In terms of business mix by segment, we note that the product segment business is growing modestly, and the same holds for terminal segment EBITDA. Kinder Morgan’s EBITDA has seen some declines in the carbon dioxide business. This is driven by the fact that carbon dioxides are primarily used for enhanced oil recovery, and that is a capital-intensive business that makes less economic sense in the current oil price environment. So, that affects demand for carbon dioxide and also affects actual enhanced oil recovery volumes produced by the company. So, in Kinder Morgan, I do see a growth opportunity with some soft spots for carbon dioxide in its current form.

The company has a significant portion of its cash flow isolated from commodity price fluctuations. Indeed, 64% of the cash flows are take-or-pay, which are cash flows that are not tied to volumes or commodity prices, and are fees for capacity whether used or not. In fact, 26% of the cash flow is fee-based, meaning that customers pay a fee on a per-volume basis. 5% of the cash flow is hedged, leaving only 5% of the cash flow exposed to commodity price levels.

So, the company has growing demand for natural gas transportation, a possible growth lever for solutions from carbon capture to storage, and methane capture for renewable gas production, which does have commodity price sensitivity. In those combined business activities, the company has its cash flows largely hedged, shielding it from price fluctuations, leaving it only exposed somewhat to volume fluctuations. This provides predictable cash flows and the potential for predictable and growing shareholder returns.

Kinder Morgan Is A Modest Grower

Kinder Morgan is not a high-growth name. Adjusted EPS is expected to grow 10% this year, but adjusted EBITDA is expected to grow mid-single-digits. The stock price has climbed 30% over the past 12 months, but only 3% year-to-date, and I believe that meshes reasonably well with the earnings growth trajectory. At 3.8x, leverage is decent, as infrastructure projects are capital intensive and often financed with debt. So, it is not a high-growth name, but what is admirable about Kinder Morgan is the predictable cash flows resulting in predictable cash returns to shareholders, and I believe that the way the business is set up, it provides for stable growth in shareholder returns and potentially share price appreciation, making it extremely suitable for long-term portfolios.

Kinder Morgan Has Stable Upside With Attractive Yield On Cost Growth Potential

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

EBITDA is set to grow by 4.6% annually, and free cash flow is expected to grow by 2.9% annually, with 2.2% dividend growth. So, nothing actually seems to be screaming anything close to a "Buy" rating. However, the reality is that high growth is not always necessary for investment. Sometimes, having lower growth with high predictability is desired, and Kinder Morgan offers just that. We also see that back in the company’s cash position, which is dwarfed by debt. This is not a sign of distress. Rather, it is a reflection of the company’s predictable cash flows, which allow it to carry little cash on the balance sheet. My price target for Kinder Morgan is $31.41, indicating 12% upside.

The company has a dividend growth streak of seven years with modest but sustainable dividend increases. Combining the upside with growing dividends and growing stock prices supported by fundamental growth, even though low growth, shapes an attractive investment case for long-term-oriented investors.

Conclusion: Kinder Morgan Stock Looks Attractive

If you are looking for double-digit growth, Kinder Morgan is not your cup of tea. However, I believe the company’s predictable cash flows with growing demand for natural gas make it attractive for investment for long-term investors. I also believe there could be secondary growth drivers in the form of carbon capture and storage. Given the strength of the business dynamics and the cash flow predictability, I believe the stock is a "Buy."

data.title: QQQI: Time To Hit Pause On Buying (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
When it comes to investing in option-based income ETFs, NASDAQ:QQQI, the NEOS NASDAQ-100(R) High Income ETF, is one of our absolute favorite choices.

In May of this year, we wrote an article titled "If We Could Only Pick One Fund For Retirement, We'd Choose QQQI," and that sentiment holds largely true today.

This leading fund produces a strong yield on the back of its clever options strategy, and the fund's choice of underlying index – the NASDAQ 100 – has tended to outperform all other indices over long periods of time.

When you combine QQQI's strong yield, robust underlying asset base, and clever options strategy that preserves some level of upside (while other ETFs materially cap appreciation), the fund is a great choice for almost all market environments.

That said, in recent weeks, we’ve put out a number of bearish articles on the market, highlighting valuation and concentration risks amongst Mag 7 names, alongside weak leading economic indicators that show no signs of life.

Typically, this has not been a good combination for equities. While this primarily impacts S&P 500 holders, given the prevalence of 'Buy the S&P', an all-encompassing investment strategy, NASDAQ 100 investors are even more susceptible to the issues we’ve discussed.

100 stocks instead of 500 means that the Mag 7's chokehold on your assets is even stronger, and simply put, the Nasdaq has done so well up to this point that it seems prudent for investors to take some cash off the table. To be clear, we’re not advocating that QQQI investors trim. As a part of a broader income portfolio, the fund still has a part to play, and it doesn’t make sense to sell off good assets just because they look overvalued, especially if you're a long-term investor.

Similarly, there’s nothing wrong with QQQI's underlying options mechanism, which we expect will continue to produce solid yields going forward.

That said, at this price, it seems sub-optimal to deploy new capital into QQQI, and as a result, we’re downgrading this ETF for now. In our own account, we’ve turned off the DRIP function and will allow distributions to build up as cash until we find a better use for them.

Today, we'll highlight the strengths of QQQI, cover recent developments in the market, and make the case that adding to positions at this time appears riskier than normal.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Construction & Performance

In case you're new to QQQI, here's a quick primer on what the fund does and why we like it so much.

In short, option income ETFs tend to be poorly constructed. We’ve rated 10+ option-selling funds since our time writing here on Seeking Alpha, and for the most part, they tend to produce principal decay and falling payouts to investors, even if you reinvest distributions.

However, that hasn't happened with QQQI, and we expect the fund will avoid all of these issues due to its clever construction.

In plain language, the fund has a unique mechanic where it purchases underlying Nasdaq exposure, then sells a unique spread comprised of short calls and long calls. The short calls cover almost all of the fund's nominal exposure and are typically closer to the money, and the long calls are typically a smaller position and bought farther OTM:

This option structure preserves some upside in the event of a strong market rally, which has been beneficial for QQQI investors given how well the NASDAQ has done in recent years:

Performance-wise, the ETF has been a smash hit.

On purely price alone, QQQI is up roughly 7.8% versus the NASDAQ, which is up roughly 40% since QQQI's inception:

However, when you add dividends back in, the performance gap is much smaller, with QQQI returning roughly 37% and the NASDAQ turning in roughly 41%:

This performance parity with the underlying index is a significant benefit for investors who are looking to transform the appreciation profile of the NASDAQ into regular cash payouts. Key to this has been QQQI’s recent performance during the tariff chaos we saw earlier this year, where the fund dropped by roughly 20% while the underlying index dropped roughly 23%:

With most option-selling funds, this price action would have led to significant bounce-back issues, as newly sold options exposure would prevent the fund from regaining previous highs as quickly as the index. Thankfully, due to the upside insurance strategy that QQQI uses, the fund has managed to keep pace with the index even during the rally.

This is incredibly positive and bodes well for future market turmoil.

Finally, on a dividend basis, payouts have been stable and regular since inception, with the fund producing anywhere from $.58 to $.64 per month in dividends:

One other important element of a fund like this is Yield on Cost, which shows how much yield investors can expect a fund to produce over time. If the YoC curve is trending downwards, it shows that people who purchased the fund in the past are earning less in the way of dividends now:

Thankfully, QQQI has seen relatively linear performance between its yield and its cost, running stable payouts to investors since inception. We expect this will continue.

Overall, QQQI is simply a solid fund that we like for almost all market types.

Recent Developments

As you can see, the issue with QQQI at the moment is not the performance or the way it’s constructed, but rather the underlying NASDAQ exposure that it builds upon.

Recently, we put out an article titled "The Market Is Headed Towards A Hard Reset," where we highlighted structural issues we see with the US economy, including AI-led job displacement, weakening economic metrics, and increasing stratification of wealth as AI gains accrue to a select few in power.

In combination with how concentrated and expensive the market’s core stocks have become, we think it’s time to take a pause with buying QQQI on the open market.

For those with straight-up market exposure, we’ve advised significant caution, particularly due to the fact that the NASDAQ's top nine holdings, including Nvidia (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Broadcom (AVGO), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Tesla (TSLA), Google (GOOG), and Netflix (NFLX), comprise more than half of the overall index:

This concentration alone presents a large AI bet that may - or may not - pay off:

First off, we don’t believe - at a technical level - that even with big algorithmic design gains, large language models will be able to achieve artificial superintelligence. We expect that AI will have a productivity-enhancing effect on users, but there's no concrete research we’ve seen that shows that incremental improvements to LLM capabilities will ultimately result in what approximates artificial superintelligence. In fact, with OpenAI's recent GPT-5 release, we’ve seen decelerating gains when it comes to model capability and performance.

At the same time, there are a tremendous number of firms building models chasing this vision. OpenAI is probably the leader, but Google, Anthropic, Meta, and many other large companies are focused on building artificial intelligence capabilities. This level of competition means that even if one company does manage to achieve ASI, it’s likely that other firms won’t be far behind. In this scenario, we see AI becoming commoditized, competing mostly on price. We’ve seen this with GPT-5's recent release, where the main selling point is reduced cost per token, not increased cognition capability.

When you combine these concerns with the other issues we’ve discussed, especially around the weakening economic indicators, it’s not a pretty picture:

Consumers make up roughly 65% of the economy's overall spending, and businesses make up 15 to 20% of this number. The remaining percentage includes government spending and net exports. As a result, with these two leading economic indicators, you can tell a lot about where an economy is headed.

Unfortunately, neither indicator is looking very positive right now.

The PMI has been mostly below the 50 mark since early 2023, and consumer sentiment remains well below 70, near the same lows we saw in 2008:

Given that QQQI is an income-focused fund, we expect that many who hold it are retirees using distributions to fund their lifestyle. As a result, we aren’t going to recommend selling off a position of a perfectly good long-term asset.

That said, at the current price point - and given the exposure and economic risks - we think holding off on buying new shares or letting a dividend reinvestment plan continue to run could be sub-optimal.

As a result, our plan is to avoid allocating fresh capital to QQQI until we feel the market's multiple has come into a much more reasonable price point.

How We're Wrong

There’s a countercase to our thesis here, and it goes like this - valuation risks very rarely lead to rapid market declines, and in the event that there’s a significant valuation premium that needs to be worked through over the next few years, markets could head sideways for a while without actually deteriorating in price.

If this is the case, then allocating fresh capital to QQQI could be perfectly reasonable, especially for those seeking to compound their income.

Summary

Overall, though, especially for those who are in retirement and need preservation more than they need gains, we think holding off on allocating fresh capital in QQQI appears best.

In the event that markets trade sideways for a year or two, by definition, they'll get cheaper, at which point we may feel as though it’s once again time to buy back in.

Thus our downgrade to a 'Hold' rating.

Cheers!

data.title: Big Tech's Credit Power Is Staggering, $Trillions Ready To Flow Into Data Centers
data.description:
Data center CAPEX to hit $1.2 trillion by 2029?

With the most recent $100 billion deal between Nvidia (NVDA) and Open AI to buildout data centers, market pundits have begun to cry foul with accusations of "circular accounting." Similar to the dot com bubble where the Internet companies were creating an artificial spurt of CAPEX related to Internet infrastructure plays like Cisco (CSCO). The end result in this instance was not a series of profitable business decisions as the internet was more about experimentation than actual monetization at that time.

If profitable decisions are being made, I am not in that camp that buys the whole "circular accounting" argument at all. One person's or company's expense is always someone else's revenue. In the realm of AI, there are really only a handful of qualified players able to participate, so this trading of services amongst the upper-echelon of tech is completely understandable in my opinion. All one has to do is spend a weekend pouring over the cash flow statements and balance sheets of the best tech companies to understand why there are only a select few companies that can be involved here. The current list of tech companies dubbed "hyperscalers" are like nothing the stock market has ever seen before.

Everyone is waiting for these hyperscalers to pull back the reigns on their CAPEX and be the first to exercise caution, allowing their counterparts or possibly even rival nations like China to get ahead. I say we're just getting started and I will explain this in a bit.

According to a recently published report from Dell’Oro Group (emphasis added):

According to a recently published report from Dell’Oro Group, the trusted source for market information about the telecommunications, networks, and data center industries, worldwide data center capex is projected to grow at 21 percent CAGR. We anticipate the hyperscale cloud service providers to account for half of the $1.2 trillion global data center capex by 2029.

Early this year Dell'Oro Group also tabulated the estimated 2024 CAPEX numbers:

-Global investments in data centers surged 51% year over year in 2024 to $455 billion, according to research published Wednesday by Dell’Oro Group. The firm expects infrastructure capital expenditures to grow an additional 30% this year.

-Much of the spending went into cloud vendor capacity expansions, the analysis found. “The top 10 hyperscalers accounted for more than half of global data center CapEx in 2024, driven largely by heightened investments in AI infrastructure,” Baron Fung, senior research director at Dell’Oro Group, said in an email.

-The three largest hyperscale providers — AWS, Microsoft and Google Cloud — are in the midst of an ongoing data center spending spree, pouring tens of billions of dollars into infrastructure to satisfy escalating demand for high-capacity AI compute. At the current growth rate, Dell’Oro expects capital expenditures to surpass $1 trillion annually by 2029.

The question here is, just how crazy is it to assume a $1.2 trillion CAPEX average annual spend on data centers by 2029? The balance sheets of what I consider the top 6 hyperscalers tell a story that market bears might not like to hear.

My chosen 6

Of the hyperscalers that I believe are most relevant, I would like to take a look at the following companies:

  • Nvidia
  • Apple (AAPL)
  • Microsoft (MSFT)
  • Amazon (AMZN)
  • Google (GOOGL)
  • Meta (META)

These companies are self funding machines and can live with or without debt. For the sake of this analysis, I would like to observe at what point do these companies "max out" their credit cards and burn through all of their cash. Typically a company can borrow 3X their EBITDA [net of cash equivalents] and still be investment grade. None of these 6 borrow anything close to that.

I consider these 6 companies the most vital when it comes to CAPEX spend in this realm as they are the companies that have the most reliance on intangible software and data processing to make their products run. In Nvidia's case, to be the data cruncher for the data acquirers.

How much borrowing capacity in the top tech companies?

ALL NUMBERS IN BILLIONS OF USD

STOCK LT DEBT EBITDA 3X EBITDA MINUS LT DEBT
AMZN 147.84 122.15 366.45 218.61
GOOGL 28.14 127.49 382.47 354.33
MSFT 112.18 162.68 488.04 375.86
AAPL 119.06 134.66 403.98 284.92
NVDA 10.27 83.32 249.96 239.69
META 49.77 85.27 255.81 206.04
TOTAL BORROWING CAPACITY 1679.45

Using the data sets from Y Charts, this is my back of the napkin math if I was an underwriter. What I do here is look at the company's EBITDA, multiply that by 3 and then back out existing long term debt to see what kind of borrowing capacity they have left based on current EBITDA. As of right now if these companies wanted to go all in, they could add $1.679 trillion in additional CAPEX spending. It's not that they will, it's that this capacity exists, something that bears are in denial of.

But wait, there's more

If we combine the aggregate balance sheet cash equivalents and short term investments of these 6 hyperscalers, we get another $336 billion. Therefore the absolute maximum spending power of just these 6 companies equates to $2.015 trillion USD.

It's mind-blowing to think that just these 6 companies could meet that 2029 demand, but I have little doubt they could and will. If just these 6 could already hit that number, then I think the market is actually undershooting their CAPEX predictions.

And rates are probably falling

The most recent Federal Reserve dot plot shows rates on the federal funds rates getting to below 3% in the next 1-2 years. While the longer term risk free rates are more or less yawing back and forth without a true direction, long term cuts look to be on the table and should move demand into longer durations once money market and 2 year rates sink below 3%. This will be a key decision maker in whether or not these hyperscalers want to unlock the full capacity of their balance sheets as the corporate bond rates fall in unison with their government equivalent durations.

And this is a profitable decision

Back to the beginning of the article and why circulating expenses was bad in the dot com bubble but not here. Whether or not software improves due to this data center buildout trajectory is one question, whether or not these large language models that can now code, make marketing material, edit pictures and create videos through prompt will be able to replace jobs is another. I think the answer here for the good or the bad of the labor force is a resounding yes they can and will replace jobs. This will expand margins as AI does more and more entry level tech work.

For example, I recently had an AI phone call to replace a water heater and had no idea until the very end I was speaking with an AI agent. I recognized the arbitrary keyboard clicking that AI chat bots use while they're thinking and asked it "are you human or AI"? It did tell me that they were an AI assistant. The level of detail in regard to the questions it was asking me about my heater were very thorough and detailed. This included scheduling.

Summary

The forward P/E ratio is high, now above 23.77 X which translates to an earnings yield of 4.2%. If growth keeps up in this positive direction, this is not completely crazy as the risk free rate, the 10 year US Treasury, is now yielding a static 4.19%.

If we look at the 10 year during the dot com bubble, you could get about 6.5% in 1999, far higher than the current yield which already seems maxed out. The 10 year is no longer competitive with equities earnings yields and the risk free rate of 1999 would have been equivalent to about a P/E ratio of 15.5 for stocks.

In that instance [Feb 1999], the S&P 500 hit a P/E ratio of 32 X with a competitive risk free rate over 6%. That was a recipe for disaster as comparatively speaking, a 32 X P/E is only an earnings yield of 3.125%, more than 3% lower than the risk free rate whereas today's earnings yield is at parity with the risk free rate.

In summary, I see data center CAPEX continuing and the 10 year yield falling as Trump gets a new FED chair and probably some newly appointed dovish governors in 2026. Trillions in CAPEX will be spent on data centers before 2029. If you're a bear, don't be too early and just compare today to 1999, there is a whole lot more variables that are not being considered.
